answer:
**The work is *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana* (by Mpu Dusun), Chapter 1 (the sole chapter, a long poetic narrative).**[^1]

This Old Javanese Buddhist didactic poem describes the *gandharva* king Pūrṇavijaya (Pūrṇavijaya), burdened by past sins, who—following instruction from Buddha Vairocana—undergoes a symbolic death (sleep) for ten nights to expiate them in Yama's hell. On the ninth night he becomes mindful; on the tenth, holding a pure mind in meditation while in the cauldron, the hell-torments reverse.[^2]

**Cauldron becoming a jeweled lotus pond (Canto 27, verse 1):**

> Now, he who was in hell, it is told, on his ninth night,  
> There he became mindful, and the sharpness of the cauldron felt as nothing.  
> When the tenth night arrived, he held to a meditation of pure mind.  
> The cauldron was shattered, its fire died, transformed into a jeweled lotus pond.[^3]

**Sword-tree becoming the Pārijāta (Canto 27, verse 2):**

> And the sword-tree became a lovely Pārijāta, all glorious.  
> Its fruits were flawless gems, Kaustubha jewels, its flowers golden necklaces.  
> Its soft leaves were a fragrant footstool, its blood-sap exceedingly sweet-smelling.  
> It ceased to be foul, sharp-smelling, and pungent; all its qualities were reversed, bringing bliss.[^4]

[^1]: *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/).

[^2]: *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-1353c8).

[^3]: *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-1353c8).

[^4]: *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-5cee32).
